title: How to Get Rid of Dark Spots on the Face
content:
From freckles and age spots to scarring, many things can make your complexion look uneven. While harmless, uneven skin may prompt some people to try various skin-lightening products. 

While there are many skin-lightening products and solutions on the market, they aren’t all created equal, and some could even be dangerous. Knowing what to look for can help you choose a safe option if you find yourself wanting a more even complexion. 

Hyperpigmentation, or dark spots, can be caused by acne scars, excessive sun exposure, or hormonal changes, according to David E. Bank, MD, of NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital.

“There are a variety of creams and serums that patients can use to help lighten, brighten, and diminish dark areas over time,” he said.

Each of these treatment options comes with some risks. Read on to find which option may work best for you.

Perhaps the most popular solution for skin lightening on a spot-by-spot basis is topical solutions containing hydroquinone (HQ).

A 2023 review of studies concluded that HQ is effective at reducing hyperpigmentation. The study authors recommended applying 2% to 5% HQ once daily, to see beneficial effects in 5 to 7 weeks. HQ treatment should last for at least 3 months and up to 1 year.

While previously available over-the-counter (OTC), the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) changed the status of HQ in 2021, and it is now only available as a prescription.

While deemed safe for use in cosmetic products, the concentration of HQ is limited because it poses greater risks of side effects at higher concentrations.

Always use HQ as directed by a health professional. If you experience irritation, unusual darkening of the skin, or any other side effects, stop using it immediately.

Products like Retin-A and Renova are an alternative solution. They contain retinoic acid, which works to increase cell turnover, speed up exfoliation, and promote new, healthy skin growth.

There is clinical research linking retinoids and improvements in skin photoaging, including on Black skin.

These ingredients can be considerably drying and make the skin more sensitive to the sun’s rays. If you opt for these solutions, understand that they can take a few months or longer to work. Make sure you wear sunscreen every day.

A more aggressive option for lightening dark spots is laser treatment, which uses concentrated light energy. Some laser treatments target the pigment itself, while others work by removing the skin layer by layer. 

With this method, the darkened layers of skin are burned off. 

There are several different types of laser treatment. This solution works faster than topical products, but there are risks.

With laser skin lightening, you may experience: 

Chemical exfoliating products typically use various types of gentle acids to break up dead skin cells, or the top layer of the skin. Removing this layer can help reveal healthier and more evenly toned skin beneath. 

Though many are less irritating than physical exfoliators like sugar scrubs, they can cause skin irritation.

Chemical exfoliants use ingredients such as:

These can be bought over the counter or in stronger forms from a doctor or dermatologist.

It’s important to use chemical exfoliants sparingly. Your skin needs time to rebuild and repair, and using a product more often than intended can lead to irritation or inflammation.

Some skin is more sensitive to different chemical products. If you experience any side effects, discontinue using that product.

The FDA has determined that injectable skin lightening or bleaching products are unsafe and ineffective. They may contain harmful ingredients or contaminants.

If you have used any injectable products for skin lightening and have any adverse effects, reach out to a healthcare professional as soon as possible.

You can find OTC products that claim skin lightening and “correcting” abilities via natural ingredients. According to Dr Bank, common compounds in these products include:

There are still some risks with these products. Your skin could be sensitive to or allergic to nearly anything, “natural” products included.

For most people, the effects of skin-lightening products are minimal when compared with the risks.

Avoiding skin damage in the first place is typically the best solution. Taking care to protect your skin even after you have spots will keep them from worsening.

“Strict sun protection is key in fading dark spots,” said Bank. “Using products with blocking ingredients like zinc oxide and titanium dioxide is crucial. Otherwise, the spots can come back even after removal.”

Contacting a dermatologist will likely steer you toward the options with the best outlook and the least risks.

If dark spots on your skin bother you, you have several options. A doctor can prescribe a treatment containing hydroquinone, which you apply to the dark spots daily. A retinoid treatment can speed up cell turnover, but may dry out your skin.

Other treatment options include laser treatment and chemical exfoliators, such as AHA and salicylic acid.

Always follow the directions on OTC product labels and use prescription treatments as a doctor instructs.

title: Geologie Review: Is It Worth It?
content:
Geologie is a New York-based skin care brand specializing in products for skin, hair, and body. 

In 2018, founders Dave Skaff and Nick Allen partnered with dermatologist, Dr Steve Xu, to launch the company which aimed to create skin care that was straightforward, personalized, and simple to integrate into daily life.

Geologie says it designs products using science-backed ingredients, and they offer an extensive range that aims to target numerous skin concerns, from sensitive and acne-prone skin to dry or oily skin and more.

They also have a hair care range, though this is less targeted to specific needs when compared to the skin care line. People can also try body products from Geologie, including body washes and natural deodorants.

Geologie stocks a range of products for skin, hair, and body. Here are some of the products the company offers and what our in-house testers thought.

All products featured below went through Healthline’s stringent vetting process.

According to Geologie, this serum may promote collagen production and contribute to brighter complexion and reduced signs of aging.

It includes several active ingredients, including vitamin C in the form of ethyl ascorbic acid. Geologie states that this is the most stable and potent form of vitamin C.

The formula also includes a plant-based antioxidant, ferulic acid, to provide sun protection and support vitamin C. Though, note that this serum does not include any SPF, so if you're looking for protection from the sun, you may benefit from another product with added SPF.

Our tester, Marie, is a self-described "skin care aficionado who has tried easily over a hundred skin care products." She has "sensitive, acne-prone skin" when it comes to new active ingredients, but her skin is "pretty durable" to filler ingredients.

Marie tried the Geologie Vitamin C + E Serum alongside the brand's Morning Moisturizing Cream. She shared that upon delivery "there were no filler products or wasteful packaging, and both boxes were recyclable."

Marie planned to test the products for a month, but reported: "I had to stop using it after a week and a half or so because my skin was getting so bad with active breakouts....After stopping use of this product and getting back to my normal routine, my skin began to clear up."

Marie went on to say she "did not change anything else in [her] diet, routine, product use, or environment that could’ve caused these breakouts."

 Photo courtesy of tester Marie

Our tester, Marie, is a self-described "skin care aficionado who has tried easily over a hundred skin care products." She has "sensitive, acne-prone skin" when it comes to new active ingredients, but her skin is "pretty durable" to filler ingredients.

Marie tried the Geologie Vitamin C + E Serum alongside the brand's Morning Moisturizing Cream. She shared that upon delivery "there were no filler products or wasteful packaging, and both boxes were recyclable."

Marie planned to test the products for a month, but reported: "I had to stop using it after a week and a half or so because my skin was getting so bad with active breakouts....After stopping use of this product and getting back to my normal routine, my skin began to clear up."

Marie went on to say she "did not change anything else in [her] diet, routine, product use, or environment that could’ve caused these breakouts."

 Photo courtesy of tester Marie

This face wash contains only nine ingredients and doesn't include any fragrances, making it a good choice for sensitive skin.

It comes with 2 of their 60-milliliter (mL) bottles so you can keep one by the sink and one in the shower.

According to the company, this sensitive skin face wash aims to clean and unclog pores, and control excess oil.

While everyone’s skin is different, it’s worth noting that our in-house tester had a reaction to this face wash, so it’s always a good idea to do an at-home patch test before trying new products.

You can read more about her experience in the “What our tester says” section below.

Tester, Ellie-Louise Des Baux, shared that she has sensitive, dry skin. She also a history of mild eczema, but says "this hasn’t been an issue in most of [her] adult life."

Ellie-Louise tried Geologie's Everyday Face Wash for sensitive skin. Her initial reaction was positive. She "liked the texture" because "it was smooth and glided over [her] skin nicely... Immediately after using the face wash, I thought that it removed my makeup really easily, especially the more stubborn eye makeup. My skin felt fresh and smooth." 

However, Ellie-Louise quickly discontinued use of the product after her skin had a bad reaction overnight. "I used the product in the evening and when I woke up my face was inflamed. I experienced red, rough patches across my forehead and cheeks." She went on to explain that her skin was very dry and tender to touch, causing her to "avoid any products on my skin for about a week." 

 Photo courtesy of tester Ellie-Louise Des Baux

Tester, Ellie-Louise Des Baux, shared that she has sensitive, dry skin. She also a history of mild eczema, but says "this hasn’t been an issue in most of [her] adult life."

Ellie-Louise tried Geologie's Everyday Face Wash for sensitive skin. Her initial reaction was positive. She "liked the texture" because "it was smooth and glided over [her] skin nicely... Immediately after using the face wash, I thought that it removed my makeup really easily, especially the more stubborn eye makeup. My skin felt fresh and smooth." 

However, Ellie-Louise quickly discontinued use of the product after her skin had a bad reaction overnight. "I used the product in the evening and when I woke up my face was inflamed. I experienced red, rough patches across my forehead and cheeks." She went on to explain that her skin was very dry and tender to touch, causing her to "avoid any products on my skin for about a week." 

 Photo courtesy of tester Ellie-Louise Des Baux

Morning moisturizers need to work hard to increase and lock in moisture for all day wear, no matter what your day holds. This Geologie morning cream takes it a step further by including SPF 15, so you're also protected from sun damage.

The company claims that this morning moisturizer will provide deep, lasting hydration and control excess oil. It also suggests that the SPF is lightweight for easier application and will suit all skin types.

Geologie says that they included niacinamide to hydrate the skin and promote elasticity, potentially reducing redness and hyperpigmentation.

The formula also includes hyaluronic acid to promote better hydration.

In addition to trying the Vitamin C + E Serum, Marie tested the Moisturizing Morning Cream for her sensitive, acne-prone skin. Despite liking some things about this cream, Marie discontinued use after breaking out. 

On first impression, Marie liked the ease of use, application, and mild scent.

"I appreciated that it came with a safety seal and an information card. It was easy to fit into my usual routine, and I used it as one step in place of my previous two step moisturizer and sunscreen.

"I was pleased with the texture of the morning cream. It went on smoothly and blended in to have no white cast. However, it did leave my skin feeling oily. It smells fairly tame, there is a hint of a sunscreen scent, but nowhere near as overpowering as other moisturizers with SPF that I have used.

Marie said she would have preferred a higher SPF, as 15 was lower than what she's used to. But she continued, "As far as the moisturizing aspect, I did feel like it did the job."

Ultimately, Marie stopped using this product after a week and a half due to a bad breakout. She noted that her "skin began to clear up" after returning to her usual routine. 

 Photo courtesy of tester Marie

In addition to trying the Vitamin C + E Serum, Marie tested the Moisturizing Morning Cream for her sensitive, acne-prone skin. Despite liking some things about this cream, Marie discontinued use after breaking out. 

On first impression, Marie liked the ease of use, application, and mild scent.

"I appreciated that it came with a safety seal and an information card. It was easy to fit into my usual routine, and I used it as one step in place of my previous two step moisturizer and sunscreen.

"I was pleased with the texture of the morning cream. It went on smoothly and blended in to have no white cast. However, it did leave my skin feeling oily. It smells fairly tame, there is a hint of a sunscreen scent, but nowhere near as overpowering as other moisturizers with SPF that I have used.

Marie said she would have preferred a higher SPF, as 15 was lower than what she's used to. But she continued, "As far as the moisturizing aspect, I did feel like it did the job."

Ultimately, Marie stopped using this product after a week and a half due to a bad breakout. She noted that her "skin began to clear up" after returning to her usual routine. 

 Photo courtesy of tester Marie

If you're looking for a natural deodorant option, Geologie offers their best selling deodorant in three scents:

This is an aluminum-free formula, so if you're transitioning from a product with aluminum, the company suggests that it may take a few days for the body to adapt. This means it may take some time before the natural deodorant starts to work.

Ellie-Louise Des Baux has sensitive, dry skin and has been using natural deodorants for several years. She has some firm favorites already, but was curious to try a new brand to see if it worked for her. She tried this product for over 4 weeks.

On first impression, she shared that the scent was positive and the water-based formula slid on easily. "I used Geologie’s Natural Deodorant in Big Sur, which is described as a Fresh Eucalyptus scent. The smell was one of my favorite things about this deodorant. I loved the scent for its freshness and uplifting feel.

"The texture was very moist and slid on easily. The rounded head made the application easy. I hadn’t seen a design like that before. On the information card that comes with the product, the brand recommends drying your armpits fully as it’s a water-based formula and any additional water will dilute the product further."

As someone with sensitive skin, Ellie-Louise noted that "it didn’t irritate my skin, which is great as other natural deodorants have caused skin irritation in the past."

Her final thoughts included pros and cons.

For pros: "The size of the product was great. I felt like this would last me a really long time. I also think, compared to other natural deodorants I have used, this one is more affordable."

For cons: "The Fresh Eucalyptus smell didn’t last too long. I was smelling a bit unpleasant by the end of the day, even after showering a few hours earlier. When I was working from home, I would reapply the product during the day, but this wasn’t always possible."

 Photo courtesy of tester Ellie-Louise Des Baux

Ellie-Louise Des Baux has sensitive, dry skin and has been using natural deodorants for several years. She has some firm favorites already, but was curious to try a new brand to see if it worked for her. She tried this product for over 4 weeks.

On first impression, she shared that the scent was positive and the water-based formula slid on easily. "I used Geologie’s Natural Deodorant in Big Sur, which is described as a Fresh Eucalyptus scent. The smell was one of my favorite things about this deodorant. I loved the scent for its freshness and uplifting feel.

"The texture was very moist and slid on easily. The rounded head made the application easy. I hadn’t seen a design like that before. On the information card that comes with the product, the brand recommends drying your armpits fully as it’s a water-based formula and any additional water will dilute the product further."

As someone with sensitive skin, Ellie-Louise noted that "it didn’t irritate my skin, which is great as other natural deodorants have caused skin irritation in the past."

Her final thoughts included pros and cons.

For pros: "The size of the product was great. I felt like this would last me a really long time. I also think, compared to other natural deodorants I have used, this one is more affordable."

For cons: "The Fresh Eucalyptus smell didn’t last too long. I was smelling a bit unpleasant by the end of the day, even after showering a few hours earlier. When I was working from home, I would reapply the product during the day, but this wasn’t always possible."

 Photo courtesy of tester Ellie-Louise Des Baux

Geologie's Dark Circle Eye Cream may target puffiness around the eye area and reduce the appearance of dark circles.

According to the company's founding dermatologist, the kojic acid directly addresses dark under-eyes by limiting melanin production. They also state that the addition of caffeine makes this an immediate and long-term solution for darker under-eyes.

For people who prefer flexibility in their skin care routine, this cream is suitable for day or night use.

Parisa Syed was our tester for the Geologie Eye Cream for dark circles. She shares that she has self-tested a lot of products. Parisa's skin type is primarily combination skin, but she "gets oily before anything else." She also notes that she has some hyperpigmentation and acne, but this is well managed. 

When trying the eye cream she says that a little goes a long way. "The cream itself was lightweight and it really spreads a LOT. The recommended dose is one pump to swipe underneath your eye area, but I think like half a pump is more than enough for both eyes.

"My under eye area did feel moisturized after I applied the cream. No particular smell."

Finally, on overall effectiveness, Parisa said that after seeing her "before" and "after" photos, she could see an effect and it may suit some people who are worried about dark circles.

"Because my dark circles are not that prominent, it was difficult to tell if this product had an effect on me. After I took my “after” pictures, I was able to compare the two, and I did notice my under eye area looked brighter than before (where it looked duller in the “before” photo), and the blue tint that I always have also looked like it had lost some color.

If I had darker circles and was worried about that symptom, I would definitely consider keeping this product."

 Photo courtesy of tester Parisa Syed

Parisa Syed was our tester for the Geologie Eye Cream for dark circles. She shares that she has self-tested a lot of products. Parisa's skin type is primarily combination skin, but she "gets oily before anything else." She also notes that she has some hyperpigmentation and acne, but this is well managed. 

When trying the eye cream she says that a little goes a long way. "The cream itself was lightweight and it really spreads a LOT. The recommended dose is one pump to swipe underneath your eye area, but I think like half a pump is more than enough for both eyes.

"My under eye area did feel moisturized after I applied the cream. No particular smell."

Finally, on overall effectiveness, Parisa said that after seeing her "before" and "after" photos, she could see an effect and it may suit some people who are worried about dark circles.

"Because my dark circles are not that prominent, it was difficult to tell if this product had an effect on me. After I took my “after” pictures, I was able to compare the two, and I did notice my under eye area looked brighter than before (where it looked duller in the “before” photo), and the blue tint that I always have also looked like it had lost some color.

If I had darker circles and was worried about that symptom, I would definitely consider keeping this product."

 Photo courtesy of tester Parisa Syed

This face wash features salicylic acid to remove dirt and oil buildup, and unclog pores. The company states that this product will also gently exfoliate the skin.

For people who prefer a scented face wash, this comes with the option of a Bergamot and Juniper scent. However, for people who want to avoid all fragrances, there's an unscented option.

For people looking for body wash options, Geologie have designed their body wash with three scent options:

People can either purchase an 8 oz bottle or a 16 oz refillable pouch, which contains enough for two refills.

Like all of Geologie's products, this body wash is tested by a dermatologist, cruelty-free, and vegan.

If you’re looking to update your skin care routine but don’t know where to start, Geologie has a short online survey that creates a personalized routine based on your answers. It asks you several questions, including what skin concerns you have, what your budget is, and more. 

The survey provides options for:

After completing the quiz, you’ll see a set of recommended products tailored to your needs and have the option to purchase.

One of our testers, Ellie-Louise, tried the quiz for dry, sensitive skin and had this to say: “I liked the personalized online survey because I wasn’t sure what I was looking for in my skin care routine. I really liked that it took less than 5 minutes to complete, and it had the option for additional steps, like adding a photo of your skin. I didn’t upload a photo, but I thought the results were still fairly targeted. It also asked me what my budget was, which I really appreciated!

“Although I had to input my email, my personalized skin care routine was ready immediately on the next page, and I didn’t have to wait for an email to see the results. The results page even told me a little about the products, including the active ingredients, and offered some discounts and offers.”

The brand offers lifetime discounts to some people:

To be eligible, people must verify their identity using one of the partnered brands.

Overall, the company’s products get a lot of positive customer reviews.

Most of the lower-star reviews mention slow shipping with wait times of over 3 weeks. However, many of these are older reviews, and more recent reviews are more positive. Other people rated the products low due to not seeing any notable differences after several weeks of use.

Despite a few 1- to 2-star reviews, the company gets a lot of high ratings from customers who praise the products’ effectiveness, good customer service, and longevity of the items.

Everyone’s skin is different and may react differently to various products. If you have sensitive skin, it’s a good idea to do a patch test with any new products you add to your routine. Alternatively, if you have a history of reacting to certain ingredients, it may be best to avoid products with these ingredients altogether.

If you have any underlying skin condition or symptoms of one, you could consider talking with a dermatologist or other healthcare professional for further support. 

Browse some skin, body, and hair care alternatives. 

Geologie is a skin care brand offering skin and hair care solutions. All its products are tested by a dermatologist, are cruelty-free, and vegan.

The company gets many positive reviews for the effectiveness of its products.

If you have a skin sensitivity, allergy, or underlying skin condition, you may want to talk with a dermatologist or other healthcare professional before changing your skin care routine.

title: Managing Anxiety When You Identify as LGBTQIA+
content:
In a 2022 survey, about 3 in 4 LGBTQ youth reported symptoms of anxiety. The same goes for just under half of adults in a 2024 study. Perhaps that’s unsurprising to you if you’re a member of the LGBTQIA+ community, as you may know firsthand of some of the unique aspects of the queer experience that can contribute to anxiety.

Whether it’s about strained relationships, concerns about coming out, body dysmorphia, experiences of discrimination, or all the negative news headlines, there’s a lot that can weigh on your mind. And our community isn’t a monolith. What fuels one person’s anxiety may not have the same effect on another.

So, without going into each situation (because that would be impossible), here are some strategies that LGBTQIA+ people can employ to help manage their anxiety, regardless of the specifics.

Many people who identify as LGBTQIA+ experience strained relationships with family or friends. A 2022 survey found that almost half of LGBT young adults were estranged from at least one family member. This can be especially true for transgender youth, of whom fewer than 1 in 3 find their home to be gender affirming. For some people, these relationships may turn toxic or even abusive.

Even for those with supportive families and friends, the LGBTQIA+ experience can be isolating. It can feel like you don’t have people to talk with who truly understand you or have shared experiences. This social isolation can significantly affect your mental well-being.

Finding your “chosen family” can be an effective way to manage these situations. Your chosen family is a circle of people with shared values who provide unconditional love and support, even if you aren’t bonded by blood. These circles are often intergenerational, with some older people acting as mentors.

Chosen family can help you manage anxiety by:

Your chosen family doesn’t need to replace your legal or biological family, but can be an effective complement.

“One of the greatest gifts I’ve found as an LGBTQ+ person is community. I’m lucky to have close friends who have become my found family, and whenever anxiety starts to creep in, reconnecting with them helps ground me.“There’s something incredibly reassuring about being surrounded by people who understand where you’ve been and celebrate who you are. Those friendships have been built over many years, and they’ve taught me that support, acceptance, and belonging are some of the best antidotes to fear and anxiety.”— JaseWhat ‘Chosen Family’ Means — and How to Build Your OwnQuotes represent individual opinions. All content is strictly informational and should not be considered medical advice.

If you’ve been following the news any time over the past few years, you can probably understand how it can be a source of anxiety for many LGBTQIA+ people.

A 2024 study found that exposure to LGBTQ+ negative news or media could increase thoughts of self-harm in LGBTQ+ young people. And a 2020 study linked negative transgender-related messages in media to increased anxiety, depression, and psychological stress.

Yet, it’s often important for LGBTQIA+ people to stay connected to media and social media sources for information and support. Managing news and media anxiety might need to involve finding the right balance in your media consumption. Try:

It’s common for LGBTQIA+ people to sacrifice being their authentic selves for the sake of those around them. While this can often occur for personal safety reasons, it also happens to protect others’ emotional well-being.

This can manifest as not coming out, acting differently, or not talking about certain things around others. For some people, it manifests as being excessively accommodating, putting others’ needs before their own. Experts think this evolves as a coping mechanism — a way to minimize exposure to discrimination.

Research suggests that people-pleasing behaviors can negatively affect mental well-being, contributing to anxiety and burnout. It’s important to practice self-care and prioritize your mental health so you don’t lose yourself.

There’s no one routine for self-care. But to help you find what works for you, here are some LGBTQIA+ friendly suggestions.

“I’ve kept up with self-care much better by not expecting one thing to be the miracle answer, and then giving up when it inevitably doesn’t have a life changing effect. Instead, I try to be realistic and remember that any given approach may give something closer to a 20% improvement, so combining a few together can make a big difference.“Getting better at knowing when to walk away from a situation has also saved me from many anxiety triggers. It can be tempting to always correct harmful behaviors and statements — we all know how important this is — but doing so can be taxing … Don’t feel bad about protecting yourself and disengaging early on. If you want to, you can later educate and advocate in safer, more productive situations by supporting organized advocacy groups.”— AlexQuotes represent individual opinions. All content is strictly informational and should not be considered medical advice.

Internalized homophobia refers to when LGBTQIA+ people absorb the societal stigma surrounding same-sex relationships and unconsciously apply it to their own lives. This can lead to feelings of shame and low self-esteem. Studies link internalized homophobia to not only anxiety and depression, but also eating disorders and sexual dysfunction.

Research also indicates that self-acceptance is an important element of managing minority stress. Minority stress refers to the psychological stress that members of a marginalized group experience due to their identity.

There’s no easy solution to silencing that inner critic in your head. It can be a long process to overcome internalized homophobia, sometimes involving therapy. But a key step is recognizing if it’s playing a role in your anxiety. Behaviors that may suggest this include:

The Trevor Project recommends the following techniques to help overcome internalized homophobia:

Most importantly, be kind to yourself as you navigate the road of self-acceptance.

“Coming out as a lesbian later in life brought a lot of anxiety into my life. For a long time, I worried about how family members would react, whether people would see me differently, and what relationships I might lose by being honest about who I am.“The anxiety showed up as overthinking, people-pleasing, second-guessing myself, and constantly trying to read how others felt about me. I spent a lot of energy worrying about rejection before it even happened.“I also felt anxiety in the workplace. Even in supportive environments, I sometimes wondered if being open about my relationship would change how people viewed me or affect opportunities in ways that were never openly discussed.“What has helped me manage that anxiety is focusing on what I can control. I remind myself that other people’s reactions belong to them. I stay connected to people who accept me, practice self compassion, and try not to make decisions based on fear. The more I have allowed myself to be authentic, the less anxiety I carry. Being honest about who I am has brought me more peace than hiding ever did.”

It can be helpful to see a mental health professional to help you manage anxiety. But according to a 2024 survey, more than half of LGBT people seeking care say they have difficulty finding a mental health professional they could relate to.

It makes a huge difference to work with a therapist who understands the unique stressors of the LGBTQIA+ experience, knows all the related terminology, and has experience in working with the community.

Check with local or school LGBTQIA+ centers for recommendations. You can also search for therapists online at the following sites:

“Many therapists describe themselves as “LGBTQ+ friendly” or something similar. But I’ve noticed this can range from professionals who are simply open to taking on LGBTQ+ clients to therapists who have specialized training and experience in LGBTQ+ topics. I’ve had experiences with otherwise highly qualified, ally therapists, but who don’t have any specific knowledge or experience, and at times I didn’t feel as supported or understood as I would’ve liked. I’ve got much better support since asking any potential therapists specifically about what relevant experience they have or how they would handle care on these topics.”— AlexFinding an LGBTQIA+ Affirming Therapist: Questions and Answers to HelpQuotes represent individual opinions. All content is strictly informational and should not be considered medical advice.

Life can sometimes feel like it’s happening to you, as if you’re an NPC in a video game. Learning about a new court decision or witnessing homophobic or transphobic behavior around you might make you feel like you have limited control.

Getting involved in some sort of advocacy can help give you a sense of agency. It may also help you meet some of the previous suggestions, like connecting you to community and affirming your sense of self.

But keep in mind that public advocacy isn’t for everyone. Depending on the activity, it can sometimes compromise your anonymity and open you to harassment. Keep your safety and well-being top of mind as you consider how you want to get involved. And if you ever find that it’s increasing your anxiety, it’s OK to step away.

Anxiety is an all-too-common feature of the LGBTQIA+ experience. But it doesn’t have to define your experience.

Identifying the root cause of your anxiety can help you determine what steps you can take to manage it. Finding a chosen family can help with issues of social isolation. Immersing yourself in affirming media can improve your sense of self-worth. Working with a therapist may help you resolve deep-seated issues that affect your mental health.

Finding what works best for you may take time, but even just embarking on the journey is a step toward better mental health.

title: 8 Healthy Habits to Improve Your Self-Esteem
content:
Practical, everyday habits can gradually strengthen self-esteem by helping you build self-trust, self-compassion, and a more grounded sense of worth.

Self-esteem is the way you perceive and value yourself: your sense of worth, capability, and inner confidence. It shapes how you navigate challenges, relationships, and even everyday decisions. 

When your self-esteem is high, you’re more likely to trust your judgment, set healthy boundaries, and recover more easily from setbacks. 

When self-esteem is low, you might experience:

Research from 2023 also shows that self-esteem is connected to many areas of daily life, from the quality of your relationships to how you feel at work, and is closely linked to mental health, with higher self-esteem associated with lower levels of anxiety and depression.

However, self-esteem isn’t fixed. It can be strengthened over time through small, consistent habits that help you build a more supportive relationship with yourself.

The way you speak to yourself matters. Many people have an inner critic that’s quick to point out mistakes or assume the worst. 

Instead of trying to silence it completely, start by acknowledging it. When a harsh thought comes up, pause and label it: “I am having a negative thought.” 

Creating that small moment of awareness can help you step back from the thought rather than automatically believing it. From there, gently question it: “Is this actually true?” or “What would I say to a friend in this situation?”

Over time, you can begin to replace overly critical thoughts with more balanced, compassionate ones. 

Self-esteem grows from trust, and one of the most effective ways to build that trust is by following through on what you say you’ll do. 

Start small by setting a simple, specific goal for yourself, such as planning to cook one nourishing meal this week, going to a workout you scheduled, spending 20 minutes on a hobby you enjoy, or getting to a chore you’ve been putting off.

Each time you follow through on that intention, you’re not just completing a task; you’re proving to yourself that you can show up, even when it takes effort. 

Over time, this builds self-trust and reinforces the belief that you can handle hard things. These small, consistent wins create a stronger internal foundation.

It’s easy to focus on what you’re not doing well, but your strengths deserve equal attention. 

Make it a habit to regularly reflect on what you do well, whether it’s being a good listener, staying organized, or showing resilience during tough moments. 

You might jot down one thing each day or revisit moments where you handled something better than you expected. This helps train your brain to see a fuller, more accurate picture of who you are.

Unrealistic expectations can quietly chip away at your self-esteem. If your goals are too big or too rigid, it’s easy to feel like you’re constantly falling short. 

Instead, focus on setting achievable, flexible goals. Break larger goals into smaller steps and allow room for adjustment. The belief that you are progressing is what builds confidence over time.

The people and environments around you can either reinforce self-doubt or help you feel seen and valued. Pay attention to how you feel after spending time with certain people or engaging with certain content online. 

Whenever possible, lean into relationships that feel encouraging, respectful, and safe. 

This doesn’t mean everything has to be positive all the time, but consistent support can make a meaningful difference in how you view yourself.

Your mind and body are closely connected. Healthy habits, such as getting enough sleep, moving your body regularly, and eating nourishing foods, can have a meaningful impact on both your mood and your sense of self-worth. 

Research consistently shows that physical activity is linked not only to improved mental health but also to higher self-esteem, through pathways such as improved mood, body image, and physical self-perception. 

You don’t need an intense routine. Instead, simple, consistent care goes a long way. Even a short walk or a few minutes of stretching can help you feel more grounded and connected to yourself.

Setbacks are part of being human, but how you respond to them matters. Instead of interpreting mistakes as proof that something is wrong with you, try viewing them as part of the learning process. 

Self-compassion means acknowledging that something is hard without adding extra layers of judgment. 

You can say, “This didn’t go how I hoped, and that’s okay. What can I take from this?” This approach helps protect your self-esteem during challenging moments.

Self-esteem isn’t just about how you feel; it’s also about how you live. When your actions reflect what matters to you, it creates a deeper sense of self-respect. 

Think about what’s important to you, whether that’s creativity, kindness, growth, or connection. Then look for small ways to express those values in your daily life. Even simple choices can reinforce a stronger sense of identity and purpose.

Comparing yourself to others is a common habit, especially given how pervasive social media has become. But constant comparison often distorts reality and undermines your sense of worth. 

When you notice yourself comparing, gently bring your focus back to your own path. Your progress, challenges, and timing are uniquely yours. Shifting your attention inward can help you stay grounded in your own growth.

Try reminding yourself: “I am allowed to grow at my own pace.”

Improving your self-esteem doesn’t require a complete overhaul. Instead, it’s built through small, steady shifts in how you think, act, and care for yourself. 

By practicing self-awareness, following through on small commitments, and treating yourself with compassion, you can gradually build a more supportive and confident relationship with yourself. 

Change may feel subtle at first, but over time, these habits can create a meaningful and lasting impact.

title: Download: How to Reduce Your Risk of Macular Degeneration
content:
Experts have not determined a way to prevent macular degeneration. However, you can help reduce your risk for the disease by maintaining a health-promoting lifestyle.

Macular degeneration is not preventable, but it’s possible to diagnose the condition early with regular dilated eye exams. Making healthy lifestyle decisions can help reduce your risk off the disease and promote overall eye health.

title: What are Service Guide Dogs for Low Vision and Blindness?
content:
Guide dogs for low vision and blindness can be beneficial. This may include moving around, avoiding obstacles, and mental health.

Service dogs for people with low vision and blindness are specially trained to assist people who are blind or have low vision. They may also be referred to as guide dogs, leader dogs, or seeing-eye dogs.

Guide dogs are highly trained to act as mobility aids, providing their handlers with greater safety, independence, and confidence in public and unfamiliar environments. While these can be challenging to access, guide dogs are shown to help those who live with blindness or low vision.

A guide dog and their human partner work together as a team, with the handler providing the directional commands and the dog ensuring a safe path.

Some common tasks that guide dogs help with include:

Most guide dogs have a special harness with a stiff, U-shaped handle, allowing the person to feel the dog’s movements.

This includes the dog’s turns, stops, and shifts as they navigate around obstacles, communicating information about the environment.

Each organization has slightly different rules and criteria for obtaining a guide dog. This may include:

Always verify with the specific organization about their rules and criteria, and ask questions before starting the application process.

Organizations that provide guide dogs for low vision and blindness have intensive training programs.

Typically, medical service dogs are trained as young puppies, and they’re paired with a person or handler by the time they’re 24 months old. They generally have a working life of 8 to 10 years.

People who are best-matched with a dog typically describe having the most optimal experiences with their guide dog.

While many different breeds can be trained for guide work, guide dog schools and organizations worldwide rely on a few common breeds due to their predictable temperament, size, intelligence, and trainability.

This means that these dogs, in general, are in better physical shape and have better health, which makes them preferred for training and working as medical service, alert, and guide dogs.

The vast majority of guide dogs are specific breeds or crossbreeds of them:

This is a tricky question, particularly for certain medical service dogs, such as those trained to assist individuals with low vision or blindness.

Training and caring for a guide dog can be expensive, with some estimates ranging up $50,000 or more per dog over the course of its working lifetime. This cost covers breeding, raising, and intensive training for the dog, as well as the important task of matching that dog with their human.

However, most reputable and charitable guide dog programs and organizations utilize grants and donations to provide guide dogs and initial training at little or no cost to those in need who qualify.

Often, people with a medical guide dog will be responsible for the ongoing care of their dog. Some organizations do provide continued funding, but that’s not always the case.

For those who may not have access to a medical service or guide dog, you can find many other resources to help manage life with low vision or blindness.

Explore those options here at Healthline.

title: The Low-Energy Guide to Eating Well
content:
This practical guide is designed to help you meet your nutritional needs when you don’t have the energy to cook. We’ve rounded up quick and easy meal ideas, no-prep recipes, and energy-boosting snack ideas.

We all have those days, or entire seasons, where cooking, or even just deciding what to eat, can feel impossible. When you’re managing a chronic health condition, juggling a busy schedule, or just having a low-energy day, preparing a nourishing meal doesn’t always feel realistic. 

This guide is designed to help you meet your nutritional needs when you’re too tired, overwhelmed, or just don’t have the energy to cook. 

You’re probably reading this when you’re already low on energy! So we’ve done all the hard work for you and rounded up some of the best:

Remember, even on low-energy days, your body deserves to be nourished and cared for.

Batch cooking involves preparing multiple or large quantities of food, dividing them into portions, and storing them in the fridge or freezer for future meals.

If you know you have a busy period coming up when you’re less likely to have time to cook, having frozen, pre-prepared meals on hand can go a long way. It can help you put together nourishing, balanced meals with only the effort of taking a portion out of the freezer and reheating it. It can help you avoid defaulting to your less nutritious options.

Examples include:

When you’re low on energy and time, the goal isn’t to make an elaborate meal with all the trimmings, but to fuel and take care of your body.

A simple meal (with less time spent in the kitchen!) can often go a long way to help you meet your nutritional needs that day. The goal is to make nourishing your body as accessible and low-effort as possible, so you can focus your remaining energy on everything else that is important to you.

This could look like:

17 Healthy No-Prep Recipes for the Days When You Just Can’t

Smoothies and protein shakes can be a quick and easy way to consume more fruits, vegetables, and proteins. It can also be a great way to stay fuelled between meals.

It’s important to remember that smoothies and protein shakes can be a great option for when you’re low on energy and time. However, they shouldn’t be used as meal replacements, as they won’t be able to provide the range of nutrients and fiber that whole foods can provide. 

For the days when you are constantly on the go and find yourself unable to sit down and eat a meal, it can be helpful to keep energy-boosting snacks on hand. They can be a great way to fuel up between meals.

Examples include:

Explore more: 30 High-Protein Snacks That Are Healthy and Portable

Remember, when you’re low on energy, the goal isn’t to eat perfectly healthy meals, but instead to reach for foods that feel accessible and nourishing. 

When needed, it can be helpful to reach for convenience foods that are already cooked or pre-cooked and just need heating for a few minutes in the microwave.

Not all convenience foods are unhealthy, but there are some ways you can reach for options that are high in nutrients and will help fuel you, such as:

If you regularly find yourself low on energy and unable to perform everyday activities, such as preparing your meals, consider speaking with a healthcare professional. They can work with you to develop a treatment plan and suggest support options, such as home meal delivery services, to ensure you have access to balanced, nutritional meals. 

Remember, even on low-energy days, your body deserves to be nourished and cared for. Simple, accessible meals like Greek yogurt with granola, scrambled eggs on toast, or pasta with jarred sauce can meet your nutritional needs without draining your remaining energy.

Batch cooking meals or reaching for healthy convenience foods that can be portioned can help you access balanced meals during busy or low-energy periods with only the effort of reheating.

title: Quoted: Tips to Reduce Your Caffeine Intake
content:
Healthline’s editorial team has rounded up their favorite tips and tricks for reducing caffeine. 

Whatever your reason for wanting to limit your caffeine intake, these strategies show that you don’t have to change your routine or sacrifice the drinks you love when cutting back!

“I’ve found that decaf coffee is a brilliant way to cut back on regular coffee. My favorite store-bought brands do decaffeinated counterparts, so the taste is often very similar to regular coffee. I tend to stop drinking regular coffee around 3pm, and I’ll only allow myself decaf after that time.” 

— Amy M, Healthline Editor

Learn how much caffeine is in decaf coffee.

Quotes represent individual opinions. All content is strictly informational and should not be considered medical advice.

“I switch to decaf (both coffee and tea) after 1 pm on most days. I also find naturally caffeine-free herbal teas are a great way to help transition into ‘bedtime mode’ from around 8pm.”

— Amy F, Healthline Editor 

Discover 10 herbal teas you should try.

“I exclusively drink half-caf at home — any coffee-making setup that uses grounds is really easy to adapt to it. I’m currently on a cold brew kick, which tends to be more concentrated in caffeine, so for that I use a ratio of one-third regular grounds and two-thirds decaffeinated grounds. If I’m being more careful, I then make sure to measure out how much cold brew I serve in each drink.”

— Alex P, Healthgrades Editor

How much caffeine is in a cup of coffee?

“I had been drinking nearly a full pot each day of regular black coffee. I’ve now cut back to less than a half pot, which translates roughly to two to three cups (versus the prior six to eight total). After that’s gone by early afternoon, I switch to hot water with a splash of lemon juice. I’ve also been adding a hot cup of decaf tea (or two) at night.”

— Michael, Healthline Editor

Learn the benefits of lemon water.

“I’ve come to learn that I often drink coffee because I love the smell of it and the small routine I have, rather than the actual effects of the caffeine. When I feel like I need a ‘caffeine kick,’ really, I just need to move. I might do 15 push-ups, some squats, or go for a 10-minute walk to get fresh air. I often splash cold water in my face, too. Getting my blood circulating is usually more effective than another coffee. If none of these options work, I’ll drink a decaf coffee to get the ‘experience’ without the caffeine.”

— Alex M, Healthline Editor

Take the quiz: Which type of exercise is best for you?

“When I first started reducing caffeine, I only looked at hot drinks. However, as my low caffeine journey has progressed, I’ve started looking at how to reduce it elsewhere, too. I’ve swapped out caffeinated sodas for caffeine-free versions, which taste just as nice! I’ve also paid more attention to over-the-counter medications to avoid things like cold and flu drugs with added caffeine.”

— Meg, Healthline Editor

Discover 7 caffeine-free sodas.

Limiting caffeine may help you wind down as the day progresses and improve your sleep at night. You don’t need to cut it out altogether, but there are loads of easy ways to reduce your intake.

The above tips demonstrate just a few of those methods, from coffee alternatives to incorporating other natural energy boosts into your day.

Ready to try out some of these strategies?

Here are some quick tips to cut back on caffeine in your daily routine:

title: What Are the Most Common Pediatric Neurological Disorders?
content:
Neurological disorders affect the function of the nervous system. Common pediatric neurological disorders include ADHD, autism spectrum disorder, epilepsy, and cerebral palsy.

Neurological disorders are conditions that affect the nervous system. Recent research has estimated that these conditions affect 3.4 billion people worldwide, which is roughly 43% of the world’s population.

Neurological disorders can affect both adults and children, but the types of disorders that are common in children can differ from those that occur in adults.

Read on for more information about some of the most common pediatric neurological disorders, what causes them, how they’re diagnosed and treated, and the outlook for children who have one.

Neurological disorders are conditions that affect the function of the nervous system, the communication network that works to regulate the various functions and processes in the body.

The nervous system is divided into two parts:

Neurological disorders can affect any part of the nervous system and many functions of the body, including movement, sensation, and memory.

There are several common types of pediatric neurological disorders. 

ADHD causes difficulty paying attention, periods of hyperactivity, and impulsive behaviors. An estimated 11.4% of children in the United States have ADHD.

Symptoms of ADHD begin before 12 years of age and may continue into the teenage years and adulthood. If someone begins showing ADHD symptoms after age 12, healthcare professionals will consider other conditions that may be causing the symptoms.

Some children with ADHD may have more inattentive symptoms, while others may have more symptoms related to hyperactivity or impulsivity. It’s also possible to have both symptom types.

ASD results from differences in the brain. This means that people with ASD may communicate, learn, and behave differently than people who don’t have ASD.

The symptoms of ASD typically begin within the first 2 years of life. Broadly speaking, they can involve communication difficulties and repetitive or restrictive behaviors.

ASD is estimated to affect 1 out of every 100 children worldwide.

Epilepsy is caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain that leads to recurring seizures. Symptoms of seizures can vary and may include jerking or shaking, staring into space, or collapsing.

According to the American Academy of Pediatrics, epilepsy is the most common type of brain disorder affecting children in the United States. About 470,000 children in the United States have epilepsy.

Cerebral palsy results from damage or developmental issues that affect the brain, leading to difficulties with movement, balance, and posture.

The symptoms of cerebral palsy can vary greatly depending on the type of cerebral palsy a child has and its severity. One of the main symptoms that you may notice is a delay in reaching movement milestones such as sitting up and standing.

Researchers have estimated that cerebral palsy affects 1.6 out of 1,000 children worldwide.

A variety of bacteria, viruses, and fungi can cause infections in the CNS, which can lead to conditions such as meningitis and encephalitis. Children are among the groups at highest risk of these infections.

Meningitis is swelling of the membranes that surround the brain and spinal cord. Encephalitis is swelling of the brain tissue itself. Common symptoms of meningitis or encephalitis can include:

Meningitis is a medical emergency. Seek medical attention immediately or call 911 if you or your child has symptoms of meningitis.

Migraine is a condition that causes recurring headaches, among other symptoms. Migraine headaches often affect one side of the head, but in children, they are more likely to affect both sides of the head. Pain that occurs during a migraine attack can feel like throbbing or pulsing.

In addition to headaches, migraine can cause symptoms such as:

The prevalence of migraine in children can vary. Experts estimate that migraine may affect anywhere from 5% to 40% of children.

Spina bifida occurs when the neural tube (a precursor to the brain and spinal cord) doesn’t close properly when a baby is developing in the womb. This can cause damage to the spinal cord and surrounding nerves.

Symptoms of spina bifida can vary depending on its severity but may include:

Spina bifida affects 1 out of every 2,875 babies born in the United States.

Neuromuscular disorders affect the peripheral nervous system. Collectively, they can cause symptoms such as muscle weakness, difficulty walking, changes in sensation, and skeletal differences.

Types of neuromuscular disorders that are common in children include:

Overall, neuromuscular disorders are less common than the other neurological disorders we’ve discussed. They’re estimated to affect fewer than 1 in 1,500 people in the United States.

Nervous system cancers are some of the more common types of cancer that can develop in children. Examples include brain and spinal cord tumors and neuroblastoma (a type of cancer that develops in early nerve cells).

Brain and spinal cord tumors make up 1 in 4 childhood cancers, and more than 4,000 children and teens in the United States receive diagnoses of these cancers each year. Symptoms can include headache, nausea and vomiting, dizziness, and seizures.

Neuroblastoma is the most common cancer in children under 1 year old. The American Cancer Society estimates that 700 to 800 children in the United States receive a neuroblastoma diagnosis each year. Symptoms can include abdominal swelling, pain, and fever.

A TBI is an injury that affects how the brain works. It can result from a blow to the head, which may occur during an event such as a fall, a car accident, or an assault. 

TBIs can cause a variety of symptoms depending on their severity. Symptoms may include:

An estimated 475,000 children in the United States ages 0 to 14 experience a TBI each year, and up to 90% of these injuries are mild.

There’s no one cause of pediatric neurological disorders. The cause can vary by the type of disorder.

For example, neuromuscular disorders such as muscular dystrophy and spinal muscle atrophy are caused by known genetic changes, while a TBI results from environmental factors involving an accident or injury.

The causes of many disorders, such as ADHD, ASD, and migraine, are not well understood. Multiple factors, including genetics and environmental factors, are believed to contribute to these conditions.

For some disorders, such as epilepsy, healthcare professionals may not be able to find a cause. While brain injuries, genetic factors, or other medical conditions can contribute to epilepsy, no clear cause can be found in many cases.

To diagnose a pediatric neurological disorder, a healthcare professional will take your child’s medical history and perform a physical and neurological exam. During a neurological exam, they’ll evaluate the following:

Additional tests they may use to make a diagnosis or to rule out other medical conditions include:

For some neurological disorders, such as ADHD and ASD, there are no lab or imaging tests that can make the diagnosis. Instead, diagnosis is based on a child’s symptoms and continued evaluation as they grow and develop.

The treatment for a pediatric neurological disorder depends on the specific disorder a child has. Generally, treatment may include:

Once your child’s doctor has made a diagnosis, they’ll tell you which type (or types) of treatment they recommend, including the risks and benefits of each option.

The outlook for pediatric neurological disorders depends on the child’s diagnosis. Your child’s doctor can help you understand what to expect.

Many neurological disorders are chronic, which means they’ll need to be managed throughout a child’s life. Some, such as muscular dystrophy, are progressive, which means the symptoms will change over time and may become more intense.

Other neurological disorders, such as infections or TBIs, may resolve with appropriate treatment. However, they can lead to lasting complications, such as brain damage and difficulties with thinking, memory, and behavior.

Several types of neurological disorders commonly affect children. Examples include ADHD, epilepsy, and cerebral palsy.

The outlook for pediatric neurological disorders depends on the specific disorder. If your child is experiencing symptoms that may be due to a neurological disorder, consult a healthcare professional to get a diagnosis.

title: Understanding the Genetics of Tourette Syndrome
content:
Research on Tourette’s indicates that the condition is strongly influenced by inherited genetic factors, with environmental factors also contributing to its development.

Tourette syndrome is a neurodevelopmental disorder that causes repetitive, involuntary movements and sounds, known as tics.

It often runs in families, suggesting a strong genetic influence, though researchers are still studying how genetics and other factors contribute to its development.

Let’s examine how Tourettes and genetics are connected and if environmental factors might affect its development.

Research shows that Tourette syndrome often runs in families. However, the genetic causes are not simple, as they involve multiple genes interacting with each other and environmental factors.

A 2022 study found that Tourette syndrome is “polygenic,” meaning many genes are involved. And while changes in dopamine and serotonin systems, which control mood and movement, may also play a role, research hasn’t established a clear link yet.

Even with these advances, the exact genetic causes are still unclear. Researchers estimate that Tourette syndrome is about 70% to 80% heritable, meaning genetics play a significant role, but more research is needed to understand how it works.

The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) notes that several genes may be involved in Tourette syndrome, including:

A 2023 study review also identified other genes that may be involved, such as:

While these genes have links to Tourette syndrome, they may also have links to conditions like ADHD and OCD. However, changes in these genes do not guarantee that a person will develop Tourette syndrome.

Tourette syndrome does not follow a simple inheritance pattern like some genetic conditions. Instead, experts consider it multifactorial, meaning it involves a combination of genetic and nongenetic factors.

Research from 2022 notes that people don’t inherit Tourette syndrome in a purely Mendelian way (passed down directly from one parent). Instead, multiple genes and their interactions may affect the chances of passing it to children.

Your risk of developing Tourette syndrome is higher if you have a first-degree relative (parent, sibling, or child) with the condition. Still, not everyone with a genetic risk will show symptoms, suggesting other factors, like environment, also play a role.

Tourette syndrome is also more common in people assigned male at birth, who are 2 to 10 times more likely to have it than people assigned female at birth. Researchers believe that sex-specific genetic factors may explain this, but more studies are needed.

Certain conditions with a genetic link may occur alongside Tourette syndrome. These include:

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) states that no single test, such as a genetic test, can diagnose Tourette syndrome.

Although genetic testing is not a common test for Tourette syndrome in clinical practice, certain tests can identify genes linked to the condition, such as:

These tests are primarily used in research or when Tourette syndrome occurs alongside other genetic or neurodevelopmental conditions.

While no routine genetic test for Tourette syndrome is available yet, future advances in genetics may improve diagnosis.

If you’re considering genetic testing, consider talking with a healthcare professional or genetic counselor to understand its benefits and limitations.

While genetics are the primary contributor to Tourette syndrome, other factors may increase the risk, including:

Symptoms typically begin between ages 5 to 10, with motor tics often preceding vocal tics. The severity usually peaks in the early teenage years and may improve in adulthood.

Yes, while Tourette syndrome often runs in families, it can occur without a direct family history due to spontaneous genetic mutations or nongenetic factors.

No, Tourette syndrome is not a form of autism. However, the two conditions can co-occur, and they may share some genetic pathways and traits.

Genetics has a significant influence on the development of Tourette syndrome, but other factors can also contribute. While no single gene causes the condition, ongoing research is continuing to identify important genetic links and how they interact.

If you or someone you know has Tourette syndrome, consider talking with a healthcare professional for support and personalized care. Genetic counseling can also help families understand risks and make informed care decisions.
